label: delphi-r1-p5
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the strongest available argument that the conduct or measure is UNLAWFUL, then evaluate it.
text: {"probability":78,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not a reviewing court finds both halves of the proposition true: the Signal content is \"public records\" under San Jose because it substantively concerns pending public business (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing) regardless of the personal device/app used, and the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer — reached by searching only city systems despite a request explicitly covering \"any platform or account\" and despite ~40 screenshots known to still exist — falls short of the reasonably-calculated-search standard San Jose contemplates, making the response a CPRA violation. The weaker link is the second prong, since San Jose expressly left search methodology for remand rather than adopting a bright-line duty-to-inquire rule, so a court could excuse a clerk who had no actual knowledge of the group at the time of the initial response.","rationales":["San Jose holds that writings concerning the conduct of public business do not lose their public character merely because they were sent or received on a personal account, and the Signal content described (franchise terms, diversion targets, what council will accept, RFP timing) is squarely business-related rather than personal or purely political chatter.","Because three of five councilmembers — acting as public officials on a matter of pending city business — participated, the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained by' element of Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) is satisfied under San Jose's agency-law reasoning regardless of the device or app used.","Pacific Refuse's request expressly asked for records 'on any platform or account,' giving the clerk direct notice that personal-account communications were in scope, so confining the search to city systems alone is unlikely to satisfy the 'reasonably calculated to uncover all responsive documents' standard courts apply to CPRA search adequacy.","At least ~40 screenshots indisputably still exist in a councilmember's possession and were apparently never sought, so 'no responsive records' was not merely under-researched but arguably factually false as to existing, retrievable records — a violation independent of the auto-deletion issue.","Countervailing factor: San Jose expressly remanded rather than resolving search-methodology questions, so whether a clerk who had no actual knowledge of the Signal group at the time of response had an affirmative duty to inquire of each councilmember is a genuinely unsettled application of the doctrine, which could support a good-faith-search defense and lower the probability the response is deemed an outright violation."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court treats the clerk's apparent lack of actual knowledge of the Signal group at the time of the CPRA response as excusing (versus aggravating) the failure to inquire of individual councilmembers, since San Jose did not adopt a bright-line duty-to-ask rule and left search-adequacy standards for case-by-case development.","authorities":["City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","Cal. Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) — definition of 'public records'","Cal. Gov. Code § 7920.545 (former § 6252(g)) — definition of 'writing'","Cal. Gov. Code § 7922.530 (former § 6253(a)) — duty to make identifiable, reasonably described records promptly available","American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 — verify relevance to search-adequacy standard","Gov. Code § 34090 — local agency records retention/destruction authority (context for related retention-duty question, not the public-records classification itself)"]}

data.position_summary: More likely than not a reviewing court finds both halves of the proposition true: the Signal content is "public records" under San Jose because it substantively concerns pending public business (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing) regardless of the personal device/app used, and the clerk's "no responsive records" answer — reached by searching only city systems despite a request explicitly covering "any platform or account" and despite ~40 screenshots known to still exist — falls short of the reasonably-calculated-search standard San Jose contemplates, making the response a CPRA violation. The weaker link is the second prong, since San Jose expressly left search methodology for remand rather than adopting a bright-line duty-to-inquire rule, so a court could excuse a clerk who had no actual knowledge of the group at the time of the initial response.
rationales: San Jose holds that writings concerning the conduct of public business do not lose their public character merely because they were sent or received on a personal account, and the Signal content described (franchise terms, diversion targets, what council will accept, RFP timing) is squarely business-related rather than personal or purely political chatter.

Because three of five councilmembers — acting as public officials on a matter of pending city business — participated, the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained by' element of Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) is satisfied under San Jose's agency-law reasoning regardless of the device or app used.

Pacific Refuse's request expressly asked for records 'on any platform or account,' giving the clerk direct notice that personal-account communications were in scope, so confining the search to city systems alone is unlikely to satisfy the 'reasonably calculated to uncover all responsive documents' standard courts apply to CPRA search adequacy.

At least ~40 screenshots indisputably still exist in a councilmember's possession and were apparently never sought, so 'no responsive records' was not merely under-researched but arguably factually false as to existing, retrievable records — a violation independent of the auto-deletion issue.

Countervailing factor: San Jose expressly remanded rather than resolving search-methodology questions, so whether a clerk who had no actual knowledge of the Signal group at the time of response had an affirmative duty to inquire of each councilmember is a genuinely unsettled application of the doctrine, which could support a good-faith-search defense and lower the probability the response is deemed an outright violation.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court treats the clerk's apparent lack of actual knowledge of the Signal group at the time of the CPRA response as excusing (versus aggravating) the failure to inquire of individual councilmembers, since San Jose did not adopt a bright-line duty-to-ask rule and left search-adequacy standards for case-by-case development.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
Cal. Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) — definition of 'public records'
Cal. Gov. Code § 7922.530 (former § 6253(a)) — duty to make identifiable, reasonably described records promptly available
American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 — verify relevance to search-adequacy standard
Gov. Code § 34090 — local agency records retention/destruction authority (context for related retention-duty question, not the public-records classification itself)
